data.title: Fortitude Gold: Outlook Boosted By Trump's 'Drill-Baby-Drill' And Bullish Gold
data.description:
A “Hold” Rating for Fortitude Gold Corporation

This analysis suggests a “Hold” recommendation for Fortitude Gold Corporation (OTCQB:FTCO), a gold company based in Colorado Springs, Colorado. The company produces gold from its flagship deposit, a 100% interest in the Isabella Pearl Mine in Nevada. The rating is consistent with our previous article on this stock.

Fortitude Gold Corporation is hereinafter referred to simply as “FTCO”.

Since Our Last Hold Rating: FTCO Stock Performance

Since our previous article, FTCO shares have had a negative total return of 6.38% (=Change -7.82% plus dividend yield of +1.44% pro rata) but were helped by strong sentiment in gold as a safe haven against geopolitical tensions and widespread bearish sentiment in the stock market, triggered by US President Donald Trump's trade war and the EU and China's retaliatory responses to unexpectedly high tariffs. In fact, FTCO shares performed not as worse as the S&P 500’s negative change of 15.63%. With the company's Nevada production prospects paying the price for the suspension of mining permits under the previous administration of former President Joe Biden, market sentiment is struggling to return to positivity. Sentiment around FTCO stock could brighten again thanks to the current administration of President Donald Trump and its promises to boost domestic metal production. Investors should be aware as well that the skepticism could not completely dissipate until the company secures some key mining permits.

Given the risk of pessimistic sentiment weighing on the share price, we recommended that investors hold the stock and take advantage of price fluctuations tied to the cyclicality in the price of bullish gold. Beyond dividends, we are confident that investors who followed our advice gave themselves the opportunity to outperform the stock market by a wider margin and perhaps even make a profit. Dividends were another very good argument for holding FTCO shares, and the facts did not disappoint: The company’s financial position reaffirmed itself on robust pillars, benefiting from rising gold prices and low production costs, giving the treasury the necessary strength to be able to pay dividends to shareholders. They benefited, as expected, from a monthly dividend of 4 US cents per share.

The Company's Financial and Operational Performance In 2024

The company has a solid financial position, as shown by an Altman Z-score of 5.88 (indicating safe areas and no risk of insolvency within a few years), as it has sufficient cash of $27.1 million and gold reserves of $11.6 million and has no debt as of December 30, 2024.

Not to mention that, while the variable decline curve of the heap leach platform presents a challenge, as Jason Reid, CEO and President of FTCO, warned in his commentary on “ Fortitude Gold Reports' 2024 financial results,” there is actually tremendous potential for unlocking additional financial resources from tailings leaching. The residual leach is estimated at 43,000 ounces of gold, which should be valued at an AISC margin of at least US$1,405 per ounce (= US$2,371 “average metal prices realized” for the full year 2024 less US$966 “total all-in sustaining cost per gold ounce sold” for the full year 2024). The AISC gold margin is the synthesis of another very positive year for FTCO. From the sale of 16,540 ounces of gold equivalent, the company generated a net revenue of $37.3 million and invested $12.9 million in exploration expenses in 2024, leading to a pre-tax profit of $1.4 million.

The Dividend Is Well Secured for A Few Years Ahead

It would not be unreasonable to maintain our thesis that there are more than sufficient funds to pay the monthly dividend of US$0.04 per share for several years, with the last dividend payment occurring on “March 31; for shareholders of record March 20; ex-div March 20.”, reports Seeking Alpha.

Let’s assume that the downward trend in heap leaching develops such that full-year 2025 earnings are impacted by an increase in AISC/oz of US$1,423 to keep pace with the upward trend in annual AISC/oz from US$656 in 2023 to US$966 in 2024. This hypothetical scenario is further burdened by the inability to restart operations due to the pending receipt of the desired mineral permits from the regulatory authority.

Since the company needed to achieve an AISC margin of $1,405 per ounce in 2024 to allow for a total dividend payment of $11.6 million, with an estimated AISC/oz of $1,423, the realized price of gold per ounce must reach $2,828 for the company to also provide the dividend payment in the coming period.

Apart from the fact that the sharp increase in AISC/oz represents a forecast of a scenario that may be overly conservative as Jason Reid keeps the wording "low-cost gold production" when referring to FTCO's cost structure, the rise in gold prices -- Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) +13.49% YTD and +27% YoY -- and projections regarding future gold prices are so solid that FTCO shareholders can continue to view their future yields with great confidence.

The gold price forecasts of analysts at major US banks are so significantly above the current market valuation that they practically worship FTCO's gold sales volume. The 2025 price forecast is at least $3,200 per ounce, representing an increase of approximately 5.7% over the current gold spot price (XAUUSD:CUR) of $3,026.66 per ounce. In another article of ours about another gold company, we summarized and wrote these gold price forecasts as follows:

“ These factors prompted UBS and Macquarie to raise their price forecasts earlier this month to$3,200/ounce and$3,500/ounce, respectively. Last week, Goldman Sachs and Bank of America raised their gold price forecasts to US$ 3,300/ounce (from US$ 3,100/ounce) by 2025 and US$ 3,500/ounce within the next two years, respectively. ”

The Outlook: President Trump 2.0 Admin. Boosts FTCO Production Targets

Production takes place in a mining-friendly jurisdiction, according to the 2025 Sprott Mining Risk Heat Map, which shows a “minimal risk” for mining and exploration assets in North America, although activity is going to conclude sometime in the first half of 2025. Donald Trump's victory in the 2024 U.S. presidential election has influenced the optimism that FTCO can unlock further years of metal production beyond 2025, as President Trump's "Drill Baby Drill" slogan, which his administration uses to promote the exploitation of domestic mineral resources, is best suited to securing some final permits.

Jason Reid, CEO and President of Fortitude Gold, said:

“We were also very pleased with the United States Presidential election results this past November.” He added that “the past four years have been very difficult for the mineral extraction industry” due to a permitting backlog. Now, he is “relieved and optimistic looking forward towards permitting our numerous projects awaiting advancement.”

In our previous article on FTCO, we described the company's gold resources and their potential for additional ounces of gold and an extended mine life. However, these figures represent only a rough estimate, as the CEO has deferred any production forecasts following receipt of mineral permits. These assets include the company's objective of achieving additional production following the development of oxide and transition ores in the southeastern part of the existing open pit, which could lead to an expansion of mine operations. Also, the County Line project and its incredible potential for 5 to 150 acres expanded exploration on the high-grade East Camp Douglas mineralization, recently updated with multiple oxide gold drill intercepts, the advancement of the Scarlet and Scarlet North targets along the Isabella Pearl, and the Golden Mile project.

While this mining scenario envisions a positive turnaround under the Trump Administration 2.0, investors should be aware that there is no guarantee that things will actually go as they wish. One thing is certain: compared to the previous administration, which was driven by environmental factors of growth in the United States, where the development of Isabella Pearl production took a huge hit, it is now not unreasonable to assume that with a changed regulatory framework for Isabella Pearl, things will get back on track. President Trump has promised to eliminate the bottleneck in domestic mineral permit applications. It is frankly very difficult to imagine that the development of the Isabella Pearl mine will not benefit from his plan to reduce US dependence on foreign resources.

There is always a risk that we will have to wait longer than originally planned for final concessions or that unexpected events will derail optimistic plans. To protect against these kinds of negative circumstances, we believe that investors can use stock volatility as a countermeasure. This means abandoning the reluctance to switch to profit-taking traders when shares are at the upper part of the stock price cycle without reducing the importance of implementing a buy rating after a sharp pullback, which, incidentally, does not seem to be the case at the moment.

The Stock Price: Unless A Remarkable Dip, In Principle Just Hold Shares

OTCQB-listed shares of Fortitude Gold Corporation under the FTCO symbol traded at $4.75 per share, giving it a market capitalization of $114.76 million. The shares are not trading at high levels, as they are below the MA Ribbon and also below the middle point of $4.94 per share for the 52-week range of $4.15 to $5.73 per share. The stock offers a forward dividend rate of $0.48, leading to a forward dividend yield of 10.11%, compared to the S&P 500's dividend yield of 1.48% at the time of this writing.

The 14-day relative strength index of 39.46x is falling and approaching oversold levels as the global sell-off deepens, driven by Trump’s increased tariffs on major US trading partners. The EU and China immediately announced swift and appropriate countermeasures to Trump’s tariffs. With fears that this could now escalate into a full-blown trade war, the stock market, including FTCO shares, is now playing down investor fears about the implications for economic growth.

The share price could soon reach a low point in the stock price cycle, which investors would like to take advantage of before the company receives its mining permits. This pessimism is unlikely to last long, as it is leading to a decline in FTCO stock. As a safe haven, gold prices could experience another rapid rise to new record highs due to recession fears triggered by the impact of global tariffs. Since FTCO's share price is positively correlated with the price of gold, the share price could recover quickly even before Isabella Pearl receives its desired mining permit. In the future, concerns about renewed inflationary pressures due to uncertainty surrounding President Trump's tariffs and other plans to stimulate the US economy could prompt the US Federal Reserve to postpone its interest rate cut policy. Since zero-yielding gold bars cannot withstand higher interest rates in the long term, this discontent could play out and lead to a further decline in the FTCO stock price.

Conclusion

Fortitude Gold Corporation is a gold company based in Colorado Springs, Colorado. The company produces gold from its 100% interest in the Isabella Pearl Mine in Nevada, the only producing mine in its entire portfolio. Production is scheduled to cease in 2025 if the company does not receive the necessary regulatory approvals. This weighed on market sentiment after the previous administration in the US froze the exploitation of domestic resources for four years due to environmental concerns. Following Trump's election victory, the situation has changed, and Fortitude Gold Corporation is expected to receive the desired approval to expand production beyond 2025 and also provide concrete momentum to the rest of its portfolio, including the County Line Project, which is going to be the company's next mine in Nevada. The Scarlet Extension on the company's Isabella Pearl Trend. The Golden Mile Project.

The stock price seems headed to reach a low point due to general pessimism surrounding the global trade war. Investors could use this as a buying opportunity, as the likelihood of obtaining mining permits has increased under the Trump 2.0 administration. At the same time, a solid financial position, boosted by rising gold prices, allows for the payment of monthly dividends.

data.title: ClearBridge Global Growth Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen, Michael Testorf, CFA, & Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

International Does Heavy Lifting For a Change

Market Overview

Led by a sharp revival in Europe, global equities outside the U.S. delivered strong gains in the first quarter as increasing policy uncertainty under the new Trump administration spurred investors to look overseas for more predictable returns. The benchmark MSCI All Country World Index declined 1.32%, but, in a reversal from the recent past, North America was the only region to experience losses and underperform. The United Kingdom and Europe Ex U.K. were by the far the strongest performers, while stocks in emerging markets, Japan and Asia Ex Japan also bested the index.

A stall in the mega cap growth trade, the chaotic introduction of tariffs accompanied by fears of a slowing economy and higher inflation sent U.S. stocks to their worst quarterly showing since the 2022 bear market. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) declined 4.27% while the growth-laden NASDAQ Composite (COMP:IND) tumbled 10.42%. Trump’s actions to reorder global trade dominated headlines in his first three months in office, sending most U.S. growth indexes into correction territory. Tariffs were just one of the headwinds impacting the Magnificent Seven. The surprise emergence in January of (DEEPSEEK), a Chinese large language model (LLM), with capabilities similar to the best LLMs but built at a lower cost, caused investors to question whether the massive capex being devoted to Gen AI would continue.

Pressured by this unwind of the generative AI trade, global growth stocks meaningfully underperformed their value counterparts with the MSCI ACWI Growth Index down 6.82% compared to a gain of 4.77% for the MSCI ACWI Value Index. Growth’s underperformance of 1,159 basis points is the largest in the 25-year history of the indexes.

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Performance

The ClearBridge Global Growth Strategy marginally underperformed the benchmark in the value-dominated quarter. While generally expected to lag in such one-sided markets, we were encouraged that the Strategy mostly keep pace. This despite our overweight to U.S. mega cap stocks.

Some might say the rally outside the U.S. was driven by necessity, as Trump’s aggressive and iconoclastic approach to global trade and foreign policy incited the European Union to action, although key policy leaders in Europe had been in discussion on the topic well beforehand. Global markets, including Europe, sold off sharply to start the second quarter after Trump unveiled a much wider and steeper than expected program of tariffs on all global trading partners, a situation expected to remain in flux in the near term.

As we detail in a new report on improving conditions for non-U.S. equities, Germany is leading a substantial increase in defense, infrastructure and overall fiscal spending as the Continent begins to shoulder greater responsibility for its security and economic growth. Performance has also been supported by easing price pressures, with inflation in the eurozone falling to 2.2% in March. Inflation in the region has now remained below 3% for 18 months, giving the European Central Bank greater latitude than the U.S. Federal Reserve in continuing to cut interest rates. The ECB has cut rates twice to start 2025 and, while growth has been tepid, the expansion is expected to remain intact.

Trump’s tariff actions have also sparked increased fiscal spending in China, with government officials billing generative AI as a key driver of the country’s economic growth and technology independence. Improving sentiment toward the private sector helped push the MSCI China Index up 15.02% during the quarter.

From a regional standpoint, relative performance was hurt by negative stock selection in North America and Europe Ex-U.K., which offset the benefits of being more than 500 bps underweight the U.S. From a sector standpoint, stock selection in health care partially neutralized positive contributions from our consumer discretionary and financials holdings.

Within health care, Denmark’s Novo Nordisk (NVO), a leader in the blockbuster diabetes and obesity market, was hurt by the rise of compounding pharmacies taking advantage of the shortage of Ozempic in the market. The shortage, now over, and the company’s more aggressive approach to dealing with them should allow for better performance moving forward.

On the positive side, Chinese EV maker BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) was the top contributor as the new addition benefited from its leadership position in a home market experiencing multiple tailwinds. In communication services, U.S. wireless provider T-Mobile (TMUS) rose on a beat and raise quarter while Singapore’s e-commerce and gaming platform Sea Limited (SE) continued to execute well. Another bright spot was banks, with U.K.-based Lloyds (LYG), Spain’s Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria (BBVA) and Italy’s Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY) all rerating more than 30%. A sector which was neglected for more than decade, investors are beginning to rediscover European banks for their sustainable earnings growth at some of the lowest valuations with highest capital distributions globally.

Portfolio Positioning

We added 10 new positions during the quarter while exiting 14 others. Trane Technologies (TT), an Ireland-based HVAC supplier, was among our largest additions. Trane has a sharp customer value proposition in both replacement and new installations as its products have improved energy efficiency meaningfully in the past five years, bringing significant energy cost savings and quick, top-quality paybacks for replacing old equipment. Avoidance of fines and penalties for non-compliance with pending energy efficiency regulations in both the U.S. and Europe are a motivation for sales while the company’s data center business is also growing at a rapid pace.

We also increased our financials exposure with the addition of U.S. money center bank JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Japan’s Mitsubishi UFJ Financial (MUFG) and the repurchase of Dutch payments platform Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY).

With tariff-induced disruptions to global trade, we believe risk management becomes more important than ever. One way we do this is through our valuation discipline. Among our largest exits was Canadian e-commerce enablement platform Shopify (SHOP), which we sold due to rising tariff headwinds in North America. We sold audio streamer Spotify (SPOT) and luxury auto maker Ferrari (RACE), meanwhile, after both newer names rapidly reached our price targets.

Outlook

International equities have long traded at a discount to their U.S. counterparts for fundamental reasons. Count among them a higher cost of capital, due to more stringent regulation, and less developed capital markets with which to fund the research and development in information and service industries. As part of Europe’s awakening to the reality of a changing global order, we believe these two areas will come into focus as drivers of further improvement and competitiveness. The current price for earnings growth disparity has created attractive growth opportunities for global investors seeking best-in-class substitutes for U.S. industry leaders, with the valuation disconnect most acute in the consumer sectors as well as financials.

Exhibit 2: Most Earnings Growth in Europe Comes Cheap

We believe our active valuation approach to growth is well suited to leveraging a broadly improving global equity universe. Guided by our fundamental research, we can target best-in-class growth businesses at the most compelling prices across developed and emerging markets.

Fund flows are another metric we study closely to gauge investor sentiment. After suffering years of capital drain to the U.S. market, Europe and Japan are seeing positive flows again. This trend is encouraging and could provide the impetus to turn the current reactionary, anti-U.S. trades into a more permanent shift of capital to the overseas regions that augment our U.S. exposure.

Portfolio Highlights

During the first quarter, the ClearBridge Global Growth Strategy underperformed its MSCI ACWI benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy delivered positive contributions across five of the nine sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total), with the financials sector the main contributor and IT the primary detractor.

On a relative basis, overall stock selection contributed to performance but was offset by negative sector allocation effects. In particular, a materials underweight, overweights to IT and consumer discretionary, a lack of exposure to the energy and utilities sectors and stock selection in the health care sector weighed on results. On the positive side, stock selection in consumer discretionary, financials and communication services supported performance.

On a regional basis, stock selection in North America and Europe Ex U.K. detracted from performance while stock selection in Asia Ex Japan and emerging markets, an overweight to Europe Ex U.K. and an underweight to North America proved beneficial.

On an individual stock basis, the largest contributors to absolute returns in the quarter included BYD in the consumer discretionary sector, Sea Limited and T-Mobile US in the communication services sector as well as Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) and Lloyds Banking Group in financials. The greatest detractors from absolute returns included positions in Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL) and Broadcom (AVGO) in IT, Amazon.com (AMZN) in consumer discretionary and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) in communication services.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, the Strategy purchased shares of Costco (COST), Coca-Cola (KO) and Coca-Cola Femsa (KOF) in consumer staples, Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELY) in IT as well as Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMF) and Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF) in industrials. Exits included Adobe (ADBE) and Marvell Technology (MRVL) in IT, Inditex (OTCPK:IDEXY) in consumer discretionary, UBER and Bureau Veritas (OTCPK:BVRDF) in industrials and Target in consumer staples.

data.title: First Solar: My Favorite Green Energy Stock, Climate-Change Fears Will Return
data.description:
First Solar, Inc. (NASDAQ:FSLR) has been pounded for price since June (10 months ago), initially from fears of a recession in utility demand as natural gas/coal prices sank (making solar less competitive for new projects), then from fears President Trump would end the lucrative government incentives for solar construction in the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022.

I would say both fears have some basis in reality. However, climate change and temperature gains on the planet are not slowing down (if anything accelerating). Eventually, investor demand for stocks like First Solar will return as the desirability of low emission/pollution energy generation continues to grow.

Solar (photovoltaic - PV) demand has largely been a function of sliding technology costs, government incentives, and competing/alternative energy inputs for electricity production.

Another positive in the future is I fully expect oil/gas prices to eventually spike. This could happen sooner than many think possible, if President Trump attacks Iran or Russian aggression in Eastern Europe expands from Ukraine to neighboring nations. In terms of long-term supply, at today's sub-US$65 crude oil price, for example, U.S. production levels will likely decline, and Middle Eastern nations will find it difficult to pay their bills, over a period of several years. Remember, including the effects of general inflation - $62 crude oil in April 2025 is the same as $50 in 2019 or the level of 25 years ago in 2000. So, I am confident an important fossil fuel price bottom is approaching.

My view is we are getting close to a washout point for Wall Street expectations around solar energy names regarding U.S. government tax/credit help for the industry and bottom-of-the-barrel costs for gas/oil/coal. Then, contrarian thinking will be a paramount positive to contemplate, as price discovery hits a bottom.

Believe it or not, the cost for alternative natural gas supplies have roughly doubled over the past year in America. If current hopes for different types of nuclear-based electric production prove illusive (especially for AI datacenter demand), and dirty energy options like coal continue to be frowned upon, clean energy solutions like wind and solar should remain in an elevated demand position.

But that's not all the bad news for First Solar share pricing this year. The latest 2025 bust in the U.S. stock market, as investor fears of recession bump higher (on Trump tariff announcements), has only piled on to the negative sentiment train for solar names.

To me, First Solar is suffering from so many negatives in April, taking the other side may be the smartest decision. We could be reaching for a multi-year cyclical price low in the solar sector, while the FSLR operating business is still hitting on all cylinders. Consequently, I am keeping my Buy rating from last year, and looking to add shares in my personal account.

Bullish Business Forecast

Actual operations are doing better than fine in early 2025, and management guidance remains solidly positive. (Of course, this was before the tariff mess, trade war began last week.)

The company achieved record sales of $4.2 billion and EPS of $12.02. New thin-film PV panel production facilities in the U.S. are being built with cash flow coming in the door each day, helped by IRA credits.

Utility-scale solar buildout is all but a requirement (in my mind at least) to match the projected rise in AI datacenter demand over the immediate future.

2025 management guidance is very bullish, with analysts projecting +31% sales and +58% EPS growth this year.

Cheap Valuation on Cloudy Sentiment

Before the trade war announcement last week, First Solar was sitting on one of the top 3-year growth rate estimates for any green energy name. In fact, forward 2027 EPS projections work out to an ultra-low 4.1x earnings at today's $128 stock price. Why invest in Big Tech high-flyers at 25x to 100x EPS in two years, when unloved FSLR is going for just 4x?

The forward 1-year P/E estimate of 4.7x is by far the lowest of any larger-cap green energy pick, outside of businesses operating in China. Granted, the vast majority of clean energy names (of any size) are not projected to earn much money in 2025, with slight income projected for 2026.

Another huge plus for me is First Solar operates at the highest final net-profit margin on sales of any important green energy business today. A function of very little debt and high cash levels (generating interest income), plus an established business run conservatively, FSLR should be your first choice in solar (from a risk-adjusted investment standpoint).

On trailing fundamental metrics, the latest 10.7x P/E ratio and 11.4x accounting cash flow number are well below average for this stock trading since 2006. In terms of valuation range, they are closer to the 20th and 25th percentile, respectively.

In my research, when nobody wants to own First Solar at a cyclical low, and earnings are sketchy (the opposite of today), shares have traded closer to tangible book value. The current 1.75x ratio is sitting quite a distance below its median yearly average of 3x. I believe this stat is quite reasonable if EPS continues to grow rapidly.

When we review changing cash and debt positioning over the decades, First Solar's enterprise valuation is equally compelling. EV to EBITDA of 4.9x and sales of 2.3x on a "forward" 2025 basis are incredibly inexpensive. On EBITDA, FSLR is trading in the 10th percentile vs. its 19-year history, while sales are roughly the 40th percentile.

Seeking Alpha's computer-sorting system ranks First Solar as one of the more attractive stocks on Wall Street using its Valuation Grade tool. Today's overall "A+" score is hard to ignore as a long-term investor. Of course, the continuation of government tax incentives boosting solar farm construction in America (and IRA credits helping FSLR specifically) will be a prerequisite for the valuation story to remain intact.

Final Thoughts

To me, the biggest variable operating issue and investment risk for First Solar is how Trump and the Republicans handle incentives for utility-scale solar buildout in America. The company's U.S. manufacturing focus is a real plus for domestic sales, if tariffs are raised on Chinese solar panel producers in particular.

Talk has been Trump would like to replace the direct tax/credit payments to buyers of solar projects, and replace them with high tariffs on foreign supplies. This would definitely cause a hiccup in U.S. demand a year or two down the road for this company.

Yet, overseas nations could retaliate by taxing/tariffing First Solar projects using American-made panels and parts in their locations. Such an effort could kill future projects and cause some disarray in terms of who pays the new penalties, as current projects under construction have been signed and contracted all over the world. Would the customer or First Solar be responsible, when this extra cost was not written into a previously signed deal?

First Solar does have a growing India manufacturing and sales presence, which may be less affected by the U.S. trade war with the world. However, I would categorize the U.S. tariff policies announced last week as a net negative for the business, until more clarity on Trump's final tariff rates and foreign retaliation become clear. The company was able to perform/compete fine on its own when lower government incentives and tariff rates were the norm many years ago. For sure, FSLR is the leading solar panel maker in the world today, outside of Chinese influence.

In the end, in my view, worsening climate change problems on the globe, paired with great First Solar PV technologies, expanding production and sales outside of China, alongside an existing low valuation, should be purchased during a stock market panic situation. There are too many tailwinds at your back to sell or walk away from this investment idea. I would rather own an A+ valuation vs. an "F" score, all other variables being equal, if we are headed into recession.

I rate First Solar a Buy and hold a growing position size. For a 12 to 18-month outlook, potential upside back to $300 a share for 2024's high trade (best-case scenario), vs. downside risk to $60 and tangible book value (worst-case scenario), are the gain/loss parameters I am contemplating from the current $128 share quote. My opinion is the lower FSLR drops from this number, the more interesting it gets for long-term holders.
